To find the characteristic polynomial of the matrix $A$, we need to compute the determinant of the matrix $A - \lambda I$, where $I$ is the identity matrix and $\lambda$ is a scalar variable.

The given matrix is:
$$ A = \begin{pmatrix} 12 & -1 & -12 & 9 \\ -13 & -7 & 13 & 2 \\ -3 & -3 & 13 & -14 \\ -13 & 14 & 3 & -10 \end{pmatrix} $$

The characteristic polynomial is defined as $p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)$.
$$ A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 12-\lambda & -1 & -12 & 9 \\ -13 & -7-\lambda & 13 & 2 \\ -3 & -3 & 13-\lambda & -14 \\ -13 & 14 & 3 & -10-\lambda \end{pmatrix} $$

Calculating the determinant of a $4 \times 4$ matrix directly is tedious. Let's try to simplify the matrix using row or column operations to introduce zeros, which will make the expansion easier.

Let's denote the rows as $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$.
Notice the entries in the first column: $12, -13, -3, -13$.
Notice the entries in the third column: $-12, 13, 13, 3$.
Notice the entries in the second column: $-1, -7, -3, 14$.
Notice the entries in the fourth column: $9, 2, -14, -10$.

Let's try to create zeros in the first column.
$R_2$ and $R_4$ both start with $-13$. Let's perform $R_4 \leftarrow R_4 - R_2$.
$$ R_4' = (-13 - (-13), 14 - (-7), 3 - 13, -10 - 2) = (0, 21, -10, -12) $$
